Q2: {"stem": "Select all brief reasons that justify answering “Yes, it is statistically significant” for this study.", "options": [{"text": "p = 0.025 is less than the conventional α = 0.05 (two-tailed), so we reject the null of no mean difference.", "key": true, "rationale": "Correct statistical inference (FoI3S): compares p to α and draws the appropriate decision.", "misconception_tag": null}, {"text": "A 95% confidence interval for the mean difference would not include 0 if p = 0.025 (two-tailed).", "key": true, "rationale": "Correct statistical linkage (FoI3S): p < .05 implies a 95% CI that excludes 0.", "misconception_tag": null}, {"text": "With n = 1,500 per group, even tiny differences can yield small p-values; significance alone doesn’t imply a large effect.", "key": true, "rationale": "Integration cue (FoI4): acknowledges the impact of large sample size on p-values.", "misconception_tag": null}, {"text": "Statistical significance does not guarantee practical importance; the $500 magnitude should also be evaluated.", "key": true, "rationale": "Integration cue (FoI4): distinguishes statistical from practical significance.", "misconception_tag": null}, {"text": "p = 0.025 means there is a 97.5% chance that athletes earn more than non-athletes.", "key": false, "rationale": "Incorrect (FoI2S): treats p as the probability the claim is true.", "misconception_tag": "p_as_prob_of_claim"}, {"text": "The $500 gap is big enough by itself to prove significance.", "key": false, "rationale": "Incorrect (FoI1): bases significance on subjective magnitude, not inference.", "misconception_tag": "magnitude_only_reasoning"}, {"text": "We cannot tell if it is significant without knowing the standard deviations.", "key": false, "rationale": "Incorrect (FoI2S): significance can be determined from the reported p-value.", "misconception_tag": "need_sd_despite_p_given"}]}

Note: Difficulty is held comparable to the CR version by requiring both a correct statistical warrant (p < .05) and integration selections that recognize large-n inflation and practical thresholds. Distractors target common p-value misinterpretations and magnitude-only judgments without statistical inference.

Q1: b. No, it is not statistically significant
Q2: {"stem": "Q2 (Short reason). the 99% CI for the difference would include 0.", "key": true, "rationale": "Correct: links a stricter CI criterion to not-significant at α = 0.01 (FoI3S: CI–p equivalence).", "misconception_tag": "alpha_via_CI"}, {"text": "The $500 difference is small, so it cannot be statistically significant.", "key": false, "rationale": "Incorrect: confuses practical size with statistical significance (FoI1: magnitude-only judgment).", "misconception_tag": "judgment_based_only_on_the_subjectiv"}, {"text": "p = 0.025 means there’s a 2.5% chance the effect is real, so we shouldn’t call it significant.", "key": false, "rationale": "Incorrect: misinterprets p as the probability the effect is true (FoI2S: p-value misunderstanding).", "misconception_tag": "misinterprets_the_p-value_or_test_lo"}, {"text": "Because the test was two-tailed, we divide p by 2 to get 0.0125, which makes it not significant.", "key": false, "rationale": "Incorrect: two-tailed p is already the reported value; halving is wrong and the conclusion is inconsistent (FoI2S: test logic error).", "misconception_tag": "misinterprets_two_tailed_p"}, {"text": "We cannot judge significance without knowing the sample sizes.", "key": false, "rationale": "Incorrect: the p-value already incorporates n; besides, n is given (FoI0/FoI1: ignores provided inferential result).", "misconception_tag": "ignores_given_information"}]}
Q3: {"stem": "Q3 (Extended reasoning). Select all statements that provide deeper, context-aware justification for the “not statistically significant” choice and its decision implications.", "options": [{"text": "With n = 1,500 per group, even tiny effects can yield small p; using a stricter α (e.g., 0.01) helps limit false positives.", "key": true, "rationale": "Correct: connects sample size to Type I error control and threshold choice (FoI3S → sets up integration).", "misconception_tag": "context_sensitive_alpha_large_n"}, {"text": "A $500 mean gap is likely trivial relative to salary variability and decision costs, so practical significance is low.", "key": true, "rationale": "Correct: evaluates practical significance in context (FoI3P).", "misconception_tag": "practical_significance_appraisal"}, {"text": "A 99% CI for the mean difference would likely include 0, aligning with non-significance at α = 0.01.", "key": true, "rationale": "Correct: ties CI interpretation to the chosen α, reinforcing the statistical claim (FoI3S).", "misconception_tag": "correct_interpretation_of_the_p-valu"}, {"text": "If p < 0.05, the effect is large and meaningful in this context.", "key": false, "rationale": "Incorrect: equates statistical significance with effect magnitude (FoI2S/FoI1 misconception).", "misconception_tag": "equates_significance_with_magnitude"}, {"text": "With very large samples, controlling α or adjusting for multiplicity is less important.", "key": false, "rationale": "Incorrect: large n increases power but does not obviate Type I error control (FoI2S: over-reliance on n).", "misconception_tag": "large_n_reduces_need_for_control"}, {"text": "Because p = 0.025 < 0.05, the effect must be large;
